text.title: REST Resource Design and HTTP Semantics
text.summary: URL structure rules, HTTP verb semantics, idempotency guarantees, and canonical status code mapping.

rule.statement: Use plural nouns for collections (/users), nested paths for relationships (/users/{id}/orders), query parameters for filtering and sorting — never verbs in the path.
rule.rationale: Noun-based paths map to resources, not actions. Verbs in URLs (/getUsers, /createOrder) conflate the HTTP method with the path, creating redundancy and breaking the uniform interface constraint of REST.

example.code: GET /users # list GET /users?status=active&limit=10 # filtered list GET /users/{id} # single resource POST /users # create PUT /users/{id} # replace PATCH /users/{id} # partial update DELETE /users/{id} # delete GET /users/{id}/orders # sub-collection

example.note: Verbs in paths, inconsistent pluralisation, action-based naming.

rule.statement: GET and DELETE must be idempotent. PUT must be idempotent. POST is not idempotent and creates a new resource. PATCH is not idempotent by default but may be made so with conditional requests.
rule.rationale: Idempotency enables safe retries on network failure. Clients and proxies rely on GET safety for caching. Using POST for read operations defeats caching layers.

list.item: GET — read, idempotent, safe (no side effects)
POST — create, not idempotent, not safe
PUT — replace entire resource, idempotent, not safe
PATCH — partial update, not idempotent, not safe

rule.statement: Return the most specific applicable status code. Do not use 200 for all responses, and do not use 400 for all client errors.
rule.rationale: Status codes communicate semantics to clients, proxies, and monitoring tools. 422 Unprocessable Entity distinguishes semantic validation errors from malformed requests (400); 409 Conflict distinguishes duplicate-key errors from other failures.

list.item: 201 — POST success; include Location header pointing to new resource
400 — malformed request (bad JSON, missing required param)
